channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Report: Wizards of the Coast is trying to discourage workers from unionizing
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 26, 2026

3 Min Read

Image via Wizards of the Coast

Wizards of the Coast has reportedly been issuing letters to employees in an effort to dissuade them from voting to unionize.

Last month, a supermajority of Magic: The Gathering Arena developers signed union cards and signalled their intent to organize under the Communications Workers of America (CWA) banner.

That cohort called on Wizards of the Coast and parent company Hasbro to voluntarily recognize the union, United Wizards of the Coast—CWA, by May 1. That deadline passed without an official response to union organizers. 

As a result, employees will now decide whether to unionize by participating in a National Labour Relations Board election on June 2, 2026. With that vote around the corner, it appears Wizards of the Coast has been delivering letters to employees warning them of the apparent pitfalls of unionization.

As spotted by Kotaku, multiple Wizards of the Coast employees have shared images of that letter on social media. The correspondence describes union membership as joining forces with a "third party" and suggests that voting to unionize could result in members ending up with "less than you have now." 

Related:Subnautica 2 hits four million sales

It also states that by rejecting union representation, Wizards of the Coast will be able to work directly with employees on workplace matters "with a much greater degree of flexibility than a typically rigid union contact."

"As you consider your decision, it may be helpful to understand how union representation works. If employees chose union representation, the CWA will serve as the employees' representative in collective bargaining with the company. That process typically involves negotiationing over wages, benefits, scheduling, and other terms and conditions of employment," reads the letter.

"Outcomes are not predetermine and depend on negotiations between both parties. All the benefits and perks you currently enjoy would be on the bargaining table for both parties to negotiate, based on what's most important to them. That means you could end up with more, the same, or less than you have now. No union can guarantee what would happen in that bargaining process, which can be lengthy (465 days on average)."

The CWA has since issued a firm rebuttal to those claims on Bluesky.

"Saying 'It may be helpful to understand how union representation works' before proceeding to spread old lies about how union representation works is unfortunately both crazy and very predictable," said the organisation. "A union is not a third party representing the workers. A union is the workers themselves."

Related:Take-Two expects to earn $8B in FY27 thanks to Grand Theft Auto VI

United Wizards of the Coast—CWA organizers have explained workers are seeking to unionize in order to secure AI protections and bolster job security. The union has also called out Wizards of the Coast for issuing a widespread return to office mandate that it claims "sent a wave of anxiety and uncertainty" cascading throughout the Magic: The Gathering Arena team—all of whom have been working remotely since the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic. 

A lack of transparency around career progression and compensation has been identified as another pain point by union organizers, who claim Wizards of the Coast has "continued to acknowledge a backlog in deserved promotions" while only promoting a handful of workers each year. 

"High-performing employees are used to proving over and over that they exceed the expectations of their role, only to end up at the back of a long line of well deserving people. This has created an environment where many employees end up working above their level for years at a time - waiting until it’s their turn to be allotted one of the scarce promotions," reads a post on the union website.

Game Developer has reached out to Wizards of the Coast for comment. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/report-wizards-of-the-coast-is-trying-to-discourage-workers-from-unionizing
